title: Equitable
summary: 9084 Contract — specific performance, injunction, and equitable remedies.
learningObjectives: Explain when specific performance may be granted.
Evaluate limits: adequacy of damages, mutuality, supervision.
Apply to land, unique goods, and negative covenants.

simpleExplanation.title: When money isn't enough
simpleExplanation.summary: Equitable remedies compel performance or restrain breach — available when damages are inadequate, especially for land and unique items.
steps: Injunction — restrain breach of negative term.

flashcards.back: Court order **compelling** party to perform contractual obligation — discretionary equitable remedy.
When **damages inadequate** — especially **land** (unique) and rare/unique goods (Beswick v Beswick).
Personal services, constant supervision needed, mutuality lacking, hardship, unclean hands.
Both parties must be capable of SP against them — if one could not be compelled, neither gets SP (Price v Strange).
Restrains breach of **negative** covenant — e.g. not to compete (*Warner Bros v Nelson*).
Every piece of land is **unique** — damages rarely adequate; SP almost always available (*Co-Operative Insurance v Argyll Stores* for negative covenants).

flashcards.front: What is the primary condition for a court to grant an equitable remedy in contract law?
flashcards.back: The primary condition is that the common law remedy of damages must be inadequate to compensate the claimant for the loss caused by the breach of contract.

flashcards.front: Why is specific performance generally not awarded for contracts of personal service?
flashcards.back: Because it would be an infringement on personal liberty, akin to slavery, and would require constant court supervision to ensure the quality of performance, which is impractical. See *De Francesco v Barnum*.

flashcards.front: Explain the principle from *Warner Bros v Nelson*.
flashcards.back: A court may grant a prohibitory injunction to prevent a party from breaching a negative promise (e.g., not to work for a competitor), but it will not enforce a contract for personal service. The injunction must not be so restrictive that it forces the defendant to either perform or remain idle.

flashcards.front: What does the equitable maxim 'He who comes to equity must come with clean hands' mean?
flashcards.back: It means a claimant seeking an equitable remedy must not themselves be guilty of improper or unfair conduct in relation to the subject matter of the contract. If they have acted inequitably, the court will refuse the remedy. See *D&C Builders v Rees*.

flashcards.front: What is 'laches' in the context of equity?
flashcards.back: Laches is the legal term for unreasonable delay in pursuing a right or claim. In equity, it is encapsulated by the maxim 'delay defeats equity'. If a claimant waits too long to seek an equitable remedy, to the prejudice of the defendant, the court may refuse to grant it. See *Leaf v International Galleries*.

sections.content: **Equitable remedies** (3.4.2) completes Paper 3. Contrast **damages vs SP** and **discretion**.

Unlike the common law remedy of damages, which a successful claimant is entitled to as of right, equitable remedies are entirely discretionary. A court is never obliged to grant specific performance or an injunction. These remedies were developed by the Court of Chancery to provide justice in cases where the strict application of common law rules would lead to an unfair result. The fundamental prerequisite for any equitable remedy is that damages must be an inadequate form of compensation for the claimant. If money can sufficiently compensate the claimant for their loss, the court will not exercise its equitable jurisdiction. Equity exists to supplement, not replace, the common law, guided by principles of fairness and justice known as the maxims of equity.

items: Equitable remedies are granted at the court's discretion, not as an automatic right.
The primary condition for their award is the inadequacy of common law damages.
They are based on principles of fairness and justice.
Equity supplements the common law to prevent unjust outcomes.

sections.content: Specific performance is a court order compelling a party to fulfil their precise obligations under a contract. It is an exceptional remedy, most commonly granted in contracts for the sale of land, as each piece of land is considered unique. It may also be ordered for contracts involving rare or unique chattels, such as a valuable antique (*Falcke v Gray*). However, it will be refused if the item, while perhaps hard to find, is not truly unique (*Cohen v Roche*). Crucially, courts will not grant specific performance for contracts of personal service, as this would be tantamount to slavery and would require constant supervision, which is impractical for the court (*De Francesco v Barnum*).

items: An order to perform the exact terms of the contract.
Typically granted for contracts concerning unique subject matter, especially land.
Will not be awarded for contracts of personal service or employment.
Refused if damages are an adequate remedy (e.g., for non-unique goods).

sections.content: An injunction is a court order that either compels a party to do something (a mandatory injunction) or, more commonly in contract law, restrains them from doing something (a prohibitory injunction). A prohibitory injunction is used to enforce a negative stipulation in a contract, preventing a party from doing what they promised not to do. The classic example is *Warner Bros v Nelson*, where an actress was contractually bound not to work for any other film studio. The court granted an injunction to prevent her from breaching this negative promise. However, the court will not grant an injunction if its effect would be to indirectly compel specific performance of a personal service contract, leaving the defendant with no choice but to perform.

items: Prohibitory injunctions prevent a breach of a negative contractual term.
Mandatory injunctions compel an action to be taken.
The leading case is *Warner Bros v Nelson*.
An injunction will not be granted if it indirectly forces performance of a personal service contract.

sections.content: Bars to Equitable Relief: The Maxims of Equity

Even where damages are inadequate, a court may refuse an equitable remedy if the claimant has not acted fairly. This is governed by the maxims of equity. For example, 'He who comes to equity must come with clean hands' means a claimant who has acted improperly in relation to the contract will be denied a remedy (*D&C Builders v Rees*). Another bar is 'Delay defeats equity' (laches), where an unreasonable delay in seeking a remedy may cause it to be refused (*Leaf v International Galleries*). Furthermore, the court will not grant a remedy that requires constant supervision or where there is a lack of mutuality (i.e., the remedy would not have been available to the other party).

items: A claimant must have 'clean hands' and not have acted inequitably themselves.
Unreasonable delay (laches) in bringing a claim can be a bar to relief.
Remedies will be refused if they require constant court supervision.
The principle of mutuality requires that the remedy could, in theory, be available to both parties.

sections.content: In an exam problem question, first establish if there is a breach of contract. Then, assess whether damages would be an adequate remedy. If not, explain why (e.g., unique subject matter) and consider specific performance or an injunction. Always conclude by discussing any potential bars to the remedy, such as 'clean hands' or delay, using case law to support your analysis.

sections.question: S agrees to sell a rare painting to B for £50,000. S refuses to complete, offering to pay damages instead. B wants the painting. Advise B on equitable remedies.
sections.solution:
**Damages inadequate?** Rare painting is **unique** — B cannot buy identical substitute on open market. **Damages insufficient** — equitable remedy appropriate.

**Specific performance:** Court may **order S to transfer painting** to B upon payment of £50,000 — SP granted for **unique chattels** where damages inadequate (*Falcke v Gray* principles).

**Discretion:** B has **clean hands**, willing to perform (pay price) — no bar to SP.

**Mutuality:** S could be compelled to sell — B could be compelled to pay — **mutuality** satisfied.

**Personal service?** Sale of goods/painting — **not** personal service contract — SP available (contrast employment).

**Conclusion:** B should seek **specific performance** — likely granted; damages alone unlikely to satisfy.

faq.q: If someone breaches a contract with me, can I always choose to ask for specific performance instead of money?
faq.a: No, this is a common misconception. Equitable remedies like specific performance are not an automatic right or an alternative choice for the claimant. They are granted at the court's discretion and only in exceptional circumstances where monetary damages are proven to be an inadequate remedy. For most breaches involving standard goods or services, damages will be considered sufficient.

faq.q: Can a court use an injunction to force an employee, like a musician, to perform for their record label if they refuse?
faq.a: No. A court will not grant an injunction that has the indirect effect of compelling specific performance of a personal service contract. While it might grant an injunction preventing the musician from recording for a rival label during the contract term, it cannot force them to perform for the original label. This would be an infringement of personal liberty and impractical to supervise.

faq.q: Does it matter how long I wait before suing for an equitable remedy like an injunction?
faq.a: Yes, it matters significantly. The maxim 'delay defeats equity' (the doctrine of laches) means that an unreasonable delay in bringing a claim can cause the court to refuse an equitable remedy. If your delay has prejudiced the defendant or suggests you have accepted the breach, you may lose your right to specific performance or an injunction, even if you would have otherwise been entitled to it.

comparisonTable.caption: Comparison of Common Law and Equitable Remedies in Contract

cells: Available as of right once a breach and loss are proven.
Discretionary; granted only at the court's judgment based on fairness.
To compensate the claimant for financial loss suffered (backward-looking).
To ensure justice is done by compelling or preventing specific actions (forward-looking).
Application of established legal rules (e.g., causation, remoteness).
Application of maxims of equity (e.g., 'clean hands', 'delay defeats equity').
Was the loss caused by the breach and was it reasonably foreseeable?
Are damages an inadequate remedy? Is it just and equitable to grant the order?
Awarding money for loss of profit on a contract for standard goods.
Ordering a seller to transfer title to a unique property as agreed in the contract.
